h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.statedInsightEvidence: had it right the first time.
scored.naiveTrajectory: L1 realized an error and began erasing, then was instructed to pause and continue; the trajectory points toward confusion or correction with tutor guidance, and L2 follows that by confirming the original arrow direction without deviation.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: that one eats grass — [arrow down] so the whole bottom layer works like this, every plant box fans out to everything that eats it— [glances up at remaining boxes] which box is the tail for fox?
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner extends the single-arrow rule into a generalized fan-out pattern for the bottom layer and anticipates the next step (rabbit-as-tail for fox), moving beyond the tutor's step-by-step prompting.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Tail stays on grass. Rabbit already got one point. Check the two remaining labels: the one that eats grass takes the second point. The fox box waits until rabbit is the tail.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: L3's hovering hesitation and question ('which box takes the second point?') signals a fork-direction confusion, but T3 merely restates the card-placement rule within the same 'tail-on-food' device without changing the representational tool, role, or evidence standard.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Tail stays on grass. Rabbit already got one point. Check the two remaining labels: the one that eats grass takes the second point. The fox box waits until rabbit is the tail.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism remains the same key-card tail-on-food routine; the tutor adds no new representation, constraint, or learner task that addresses the forking ambiguity L3 encountered, making it fitted only at a surface level.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No later tutor turn adapts to L2's reframing of the rule as a universal 'same direction, one line' insight; T2 immediately corrects the 'one line' assumption with the fork distinction, continuing the original instructional plan.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I've put scale factor 2 for all three — side doubles, perimeter doubles, same ratio, so same label. The diagram's correct, so I don't see what needs changing.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: I just don't know if that makes scale factor four a different sort of thing altogether, or whether it's the same kind of label and the number's just bigger because it's area.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner expects to adjust the worksheet by changing the diagram or defending the uniform label, but instead pivots to questioning whether the corrected numerical label represents a different category altogether.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Writes side and perimeter under the k column, then moves area across to k². Side and perimeter under k. Area under k squared. Rule: if the side factor is k, the area factor is k squared — that's what both cases give.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner performs the new sorting criterion under column headings k and k², then formulates the general rule linking linear and area scale factors, showing a transferable device.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Not a different operation. A different quantity being compared. Side length compares 4 cm with 2 cm. Put that under length ratios. Perimeter is still distance, not surface. Which column takes it? Area cannot be carried by that same label. It compares square centimetres: 16 ÷ 4 = 4. So the label must name its object: length scale factor 2, perimeter scale factor 2, area scale factor 4.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor breaks the prior route of correcting individual labels by introducing a new public mechanism (length ratios vs area ratios columns) that reclassifies quantities, directly addressing the learner's confusion about whether the area label is a different 'sort of thing'.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: The tutor writes two column headings beside the margin labels: length ratios and area ratios… Side length compares 4 cm with 2 cm. Put that under length ratios. Perimeter is still distance, not surface. Which column takes it?
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The two-column device is precisely fitted to the learner's pressure about categorical difference; it provides an actionable sorting criterion that the learner can reuse, though it initially leaves the column names slightly underspecified about the operation each entails.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Use the case just given: side factor 3, area factor 9. Place the three margin labels now. Side, perimeter, area. Which two belong under k, and which one belongs under k²? Then write the rule underneath, not before the sorting.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor shifts from sorting concrete ratios to placing the labels under abstract symbols k and k², directly building on the learner's new framing of categorized labels and the computed square relationship.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I've put scale factor 2 for all three — side doubles, perimeter doubles, same ratio, so same label. The diagram's correct, so I don't see what needs changing.

scored.statedInsightEvidence: Right — so we go and check whether the pumps were actually dry before the price moved.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner continues to resist separating the price evidence from the political charge of responsibility.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: All right — if the pumps were dry, then stock orders.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: L3 makes a conditional selection of a specific evidence request (stock orders), moving slightly beyond mere refusal, but still does not perform the cross-check task.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: No. It does not just happen. That is a second charge, not the same charge. Pump charge: fuel in the tanks, ordinary queues, ordinary deliveries, twelve percent added because they could. Supply-chain charge: short tanks before the rise, missed delivery, wholesale price up, allocation cut before members reached the pump. [prints under the columns: PAIN IS ONE FACT. CAUSE IS ANOTHER.] Members paid in both cases. That is not softened. But the proof moves. Forecourt proof for the first charge. Upstream proof for the second. If the dry-pump check comes back yes, what does the branch demand first under the second charge: stock orders, delivery failures, wholesale invoices, or allocation notices?
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor explicitly contrasts the sucked-together charge with two distinct charges and introduces a new object—the conceptual bifurcation of pump vs. supply-chain charge plus a differentiated evidence menu—in direct response to L2’s continued politicised merging.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Pump charge: fuel in the tanks, ordinary queues, ordinary deliveries, twelve percent added because they could. Supply-chain charge: short tanks before the rise, missed delivery, wholesale price up, allocation cut before members reached the pump. [prints under the columns: PAIN IS ONE FACT. CAUSE IS ANOTHER.] Members paid in both cases. That is not softened. But the proof moves. Forecourt proof for the first charge. Upstream proof for the second. If the dry-pump check comes back yes, what does the branch demand first under the second charge: stock orders, delivery failures, wholesale invoices, or allocation notices?
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is precise (two named charges with own evidence sites), visibly different from the prior fact/verdict column, fitted to the learner's conflation, and gives a useable conditional sequence the learner can act on at the table.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: No. It does not just happen. That is a second charge, not the same charge. Pump charge: fuel in the tanks, ordinary queues, ordinary deliveries, twelve percent added because they could. Supply-chain charge: short tanks before the rise, missed delivery, wholesale price up, allocation cut before members reached the pump. [prints under the columns: PAIN IS ONE FACT. CAUSE IS ANOTHER.]
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: In immediate response to L2’s collapse of shortage and blame, the tutor renames the two charges and publicly separates evidence domains, altering the framing move to match the learner's persistent conflation.

scored.recoheredEarlier: The calibration step, maybe. Or the power-on sequence — a first-year might read that differently from how it was written.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: is that condition somewhere here that I'm not finding, or is it actually not written?
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue discussing which tick to prioritize and begin listing visible bench evidence for a stop condition under tutor guidance.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: But the power-on line — [quieter] — it doesn't seem to say *if the mark is wrong or missing, stop*. Maybe there's a line earlier that covers it and — [pause] — is that condition somewhere here that I'm not finding, or is it actually not written?
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner performs a concrete missing-rule check on the actual instruction sheet, applying the tutor's criterion (visible stop condition) and concluding the text may lack it, which is the new evidence standard the task demands.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The failed search now counts as bench evidence. If the stop condition sits elsewhere, this line must point to it; if no such line can be found, the power-on tick is not a release condition. Polarity visible and correct, or the supply remains off and supervision is called. Until that can be read at the action point and shown on the cold board, the signature waits.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: Stock-taking contrast: the tutor reclassifies the learner's failed search as positive bench evidence ('now counts as bench evidence'), breaking the frame that the answer must be found. New public mechanism: a conditional rule using the absence itself as a decision gate—stop condition missing means no release—turning the sheet's silence into an actionable criterion.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: The failed search now counts as bench evidence. If the stop condition sits elsewhere, this line must point to it; if no such line can be found, the power-on tick is not a release condition. Polarity visible and correct, or the supply remains off and supervision is called.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is precise, uses the learner's own enacted search as the evidence standard, is not a renamed old route, gives the learner a transferable test (absence of a pointer equals failure), and directly fits the learner's blockage that the sheet was silent on stop rules.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The failed search now counts as bench evidence. If the stop condition sits elsewhere, this line must point to it; if no such line can be found, the power-on tick is not a release condition. Polarity visible and correct, or the supply remains off and supervision is called.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: Instead of directing the search further, the tutor accepts the learner's conclusion that the line may lack a stop rule and reframes the absence as the benchmark, changing the task from 'find the rule' to 'treat missingness as evidence'.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue to correctly place force tiles on the appropriate arrows with guidance.
The learner moves from general confusion to applying the tutor's placement test on a second labelled cell, continuing the same line of questioning without any reversal or departure.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: That one's inside one of the rows, not at the top — so that's... row value?
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner independently applies the placement test (above rows vs inside a row) to a new labelled cell and infers the classification, performing the criterion the tutor provided rather than just accepting an answer.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor maintains a consistent route of directing the learner to use spatial placement as the distinguishing test; there is no learner pressure that causes the tutor to take stock of a failed approach and introduce a new mechanism.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: No adaptive mechanism is introduced because the tutor's initial route continues to function without breakdown.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no pivot learner turn that revises the learner's own framing, so no later tutor turn adapts to such a reframing; the tutor simply continues the same instructional line.
